0: <title>Refining life cycle nutrient modeling in organic pig production. An analysis focusing on feeding strategies in organic Danish pig farming </title>
Teodora Dorca-Preda a,* , Anne Grete Kongsted ³, Heidi Mai-Lis Andersen a, b , Troels Kristensen a Peter Kappel Theil c,1 , Marie Trydeman Knudsen Lisbeth Mogensen a

<list>a Department of Agroecology, Aarhus University, Blichers Allé 20, P.O. BOX 50, DK, 8830 Tjele, Denmark 
b Innovation Centre for Organic Farming, Agro Food Park 26, DK, 8200 Aarhus N, Denmark 
C Department of Animal and Veterinary Sciences, Aarhus University, Blichers Allé 20, P.O. BOX 50, DK, 8830 Tjele, Denmark</list>
1:
<list>Sub-systems were considered in nutrient modeling. 
N emission factors for housing and paddocks adjusted to organic farming were identified. 
Organic-specific N content data for manure were employed. 
Feeding strategies were analyzed for SOWS only and SOWS and fattening pigs. 
Changes in feeding strategy led to reductions of 0-13% in the environmental impacts.</list>

ARTICLEINFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Free-range SOWS with piglets Environmental impacts Nitrogen balance Nitrate leaching Ammonia volatilization|Organic pig farming systems are associated with considerable ammonia losses, nitrate leaching in SOW pasture concepts, and dependency on imported protein feed components, such as soybean cake. Furthermore, in envi- ronmental nutrient modeling approaches, there is a lack of data on emission factors and models that account for nutrient balances concerning organic production systems. Therefore, the present study aimed to analyze feeding strategies in organic pig farming by using refined nutrient modeling approaches. A cradle-to-farm-gate life cycle approach was used to analyze six feeding scenarios: reduced crude protein content in feed rations for SOWS (S1) and SOWS and fattening pigs (S3), alternative protein sources and reduced protein in feed rations for SOWS (S2) and SOWS and fattening pigs (S4), and reduced compound feed intake for SOWS (S5) and SOWS and fattening pigs (S6). Danish average production data were collected and specific data regarding organic production (N content in manure, emission factors for ammonia for the paddock and housing) were identified. Six sub-systems (paddocks with SOWS and piglets on grass-clover, weaners, fattening pigs, feed, manure, and manufacturing of inputs) were considered and balanced out. Five environmental impact categories, climate change (including soil carbon changes), eutrophication potential, acidification potential, abiotic deple- tion of fossil fuels, and land occupation, were assessed. It was found that the feeding strategies had considerably higher effects when including both SOWS and fattening pigs compared to only including SOWS. The highest reductions for most of the analyzed environmental impacts (13% for eutrophication and acidification, 11% for climate change, and 8% for land occupation) were estimated due to reduced amount of compound feed to both SOWS and fattening pigs (S6). For abiotic depletion of fossil fuels, a decrease of 33% was determined by the use of locally grown protein sources (S4), which decreased the contribution of transportation. In terms of methodological refinements, an approach for nutrient modeling at the paddock level, where crop rotation and associated effects were also considered, was identified. Furthermore, adapted emission factors for indoor housing with access to outdoor runs were suggested.
2: Organic pig production has increased considerably in the last decade in Denmark (Landbrugsstyrelsen, 2021) but also in several other Euro- pean countries (Willer et al., 2021). While consumers perceive organic production as more sustainable due to the ban on chemicals (particu- larly pesticides), attention to animal welfare, and health and environ- mental aspects (Grunert, 2005; Landbrug and Fodevarer, 2021), several environmental challenges are associated with organic pig production.

One critical aspect is represented by the nitrogen (N) losses such as nitrate (NO3) leaching and nitrous oxide (N20) emissions from SOW pasture concepts (Williams et al., 2000; Eriksen et al., 2002; 2006; Manevski et al., 2019) and ammonia (NH3) volatilization from fattening pig housing (Olsson et al., 2014; Salomon et al., 2020). These challenges are multifactorial but largely linked to high feed consumption combined with high dietary protein concentrations (Olsson et al., 2014; Eriksen et al., 2006). The increased feed use in organic compared to conven- tional practice can partly be explained by the higher energy re- quirements of organic pigs due to higher physical activity levels and exposure to varying climatic conditions combined with extended lactation in piglet production (Studnitz, 2019; van der Heide et al., 2021). Likewise, the unavailability of organic protein components in sufficient quality and quantity together with the ban on the use of single synthetic amino acids in organic livestock production challenges the formulation of feedstuff meeting the pigs' requirements of essential amino acids without excessive dietary crude protein content (Zollitsch, 2007).

Another aspect challenging the credibility of organic pork produc- tion is the high dependency on imported feed components such as soy- bean partly imported from outside Europe. Increased regional feed self- sufficiency would not only comply well with the organic principles but might also improve the resilience of organic pork production.

Thus, improvements in feeding strategies are needed in organic pig production to improve the credibility and sustainability of the produc- tion while meeting the pigs' nutritional needs. Eskildsen et al. (2020a) investigated the effects of reduced dietary crude protein for gestating (from 12.8% to 11.4% of compound feed) and lactating (from 14.8% to 13.0% of compound feed) SOWS during summer. They found that the productivity of the SOWS was not affected. In a more recent study by (Johannsen et al. (2022), a more pronounced reduction in gestational dietary protein content even benefitted the subsequent litter performance.

Additionally, to reduce the use of imported soybean (cake), peas and fava beans represent locally produced alternatives in pig feeding (Briggs and Hines, 2022; Smith et al., 2013; White et al., 2015). While the an- imal performance, carcass composition, and nutrient excretion of pigs are not affected by the inclusion of the above-mentioned feedstuffs in feed rations (Smith et al., 2013; White et al., 2015), data on the envi- ronmental impact of these strategies are lacking.

Concerning the environmental assessment of livestock production systems, life cycle assessment (LCA) is considered one of the most complete methods to analyze the environmental pressure related to the production cycle of a product. Nevertheless, several challenges arise when applying the LCA approach in organic farming (Montemayor et al., 2022; van der Werf et al., 2020). Regarding the flow of nutrients, Montemayor et al. (2022) identified several limitations. One important aspect is that nutrient composition data for organic manure are based on conventional manure. Then, the approaches used in emission modeling do not always account for nutrient balances in the system.

To balance out the nutrient flow within organic pig farming, a dif- ferentiation between the manure deposited on the paddock and manure collected during housing is required. When modeling the environmental impacts of the crops cultivated after grass-clover (where manure was deposited) fertilization must be disregarded as it is prohibited in the following year according to the Danish regulation. Then, while in con- ventional systems the collected manure might not always have a high

economic value, it can be regarded as a co-product in the organic sys- tems due to the higher dependency on organic fertilizer. To model it as a co-product, information about feed intake at different stages, associated emissions, and fertilizer replacement ratio is necessary. Lastly, to cap- ture the characteristics of organic feeding strategies and housing sys- items, emission factors for N emissions (particularly NH3) need to be adapted. A first effort in adapting these factors to Danish organic housing conditions was performed by Kai et al. (2022). In that study, literature data were screened to project NH3 emission factors for housing systems with outdoor concrete runs. Furthermore, recommendations exist for nutrient modeling at the paddock level, where implications on rotations (the grass-clover and following crop) are considered (Eriksen et al., 2002; Kongsted et al., 2020).

Therefore, the present study aimed to analyze the environmental effect of changes in feeding strategies for organic SOWS and fattening pigs compared to the current standard feeding used in organic herds in Denmark and, at the same time, refine the nutrient modeling approaches for organic pig production taking into account the subsystems and using organic-specific data and emission factors. The changes in feeding strategies included: i) reducing the amount of crude protein in the ra- tions; ii) increasing the proportion of locally produced protein feed while reducing the crude protein in the rations; and iii) reducing the amount of compound feed used (increased efficiency).

To model it as a co-product, information about feed intake at different stages, associated emissions, and fertilizer replacement ratio is necessary. Lastly, to cap- ture the characteristics of organic feeding strategies and housing sys- items, emission factors for N emissions (particularly NH3) need to be adapted. A first effort in adapting these factors to Danish organic housing conditions was performed by Kai et al. (2022). In that study, literature data were screened to project NH3 emission factors for housing systems with outdoor concrete runs. Furthermore, recommendations exist for nutrient modeling at the paddock level, where implications on rotations (the grass-clover and following crop) are considered (Eriksen et al., 2002; Kongsted et al., 2020).

Therefore, the present study aimed to analyze the environmental effect of changes in feeding strategies for organic SOWS and fattening pigs compared to the current standard feeding used in organic herds in Denmark and, at the same time, refine the nutrient modeling approaches for organic pig production taking into account the subsystems and using organic-specific data and emission factors. The changes in feeding strategies included: i) reducing the amount of crude protein in the ra- tions; ii) increasing the proportion of locally produced protein feed while reducing the crude protein in the rations; and iii) reducing the amount of compound feed used (increased efficiency).
8: <header>1. Introduction </header>

Organic pig production has increased considerably in the last decade in Denmark (Landbrugsstyrelsen, 2021) but also in several other Euro- pean countries (Willer et al., 2021). While consumers perceive organic production as more sustainable due to the ban on chemicals (particu- larly pesticides), attention to animal welfare, and health and environ- mental aspects (Grunert, 2005; Landbrug and Fodevarer, 2021), several environmental challenges are associated with organic pig production.

One critical aspect is represented by the nitrogen (N) losses such as nitrate (NO3) leaching and nitrous oxide (N20) emissions from SOW pasture concepts (Williams et al., 2000; Eriksen et al., 2002; 2006; Manevski et al., 2019) and ammonia (NH3) volatilization from fattening pig housing (Olsson et al., 2014; Salomon et al., 2020). These challenges are multifactorial but largely linked to high feed consumption combined with high dietary protein concentrations (Olsson et al., 2014; Eriksen et al., 2006). The increased feed use in organic compared to conven- tional practice can partly be explained by the higher energy re- quirements of organic pigs due to higher physical activity levels and exposure to varying climatic conditions combined with extended lactation in piglet production (Studnitz, 2019; van der Heide et al., 2021). Likewise, the unavailability of organic protein components in sufficient quality and quantity together with the ban on the use of single synthetic amino acids in organic livestock production challenges the formulation of feedstuff meeting the pigs' requirements of essential amino acids without excessive dietary crude protein content (Zollitsch, 2007).

Another aspect challenging the credibility of organic pork produc- tion is the high dependency on imported feed components such as soy- bean partly imported from outside Europe. Increased regional feed self- sufficiency would not only comply well with the organic principles but might also improve the resilience of organic pork production.

Thus, improvements in feeding strategies are needed in organic pig production to improve the credibility and sustainability of the produc- tion while meeting the pigs' nutritional needs. Eskildsen et al. (2020a) investigated the effects of reduced dietary crude protein for gestating (from 12.8% to 11.4% of compound feed) and lactating (from 14.8% to 13.0% of compound feed) SOWS during summer. They found that the productivity of the SOWS was not affected. In a more recent study by (Johannsen et al. (2022), a more pronounced reduction in gestational dietary protein content even benefitted the subsequent litter performance.

Additionally, to reduce the use of imported soybean (cake), peas and fava beans represent locally produced alternatives in pig feeding (Briggs and Hines, 2022; Smith et al., 2013; White et al., 2015). While the an- imal performance, carcass composition, and nutrient excretion of pigs are not affected by the inclusion of the above-mentioned feedstuffs in feed rations (Smith et al., 2013; White et al., 2015), data on the envi- ronmental impact of these strategies are lacking.

Concerning the environmental assessment of livestock production systems, life cycle assessment (LCA) is considered one of the most complete methods to analyze the environmental pressure related to the production cycle of a product. Nevertheless, several challenges arise when applying the LCA approach in organic farming (Montemayor et al., 2022; van der Werf et al., 2020). Regarding the flow of nutrients, Montemayor et al. (2022) identified several limitations. One important aspect is that nutrient composition data for organic manure are based on conventional manure. Then, the approaches used in emission modeling do not always account for nutrient balances in the system.

To balance out the nutrient flow within organic pig farming, a dif- ferentiation between the manure deposited on the paddock and manure collected during housing is required. When modeling the environmental impacts of the crops cultivated after grass-clover (where manure was deposited) fertilization must be disregarded as it is prohibited in the following year according to the Danish regulation. Then, while in con- ventional systems the collected manure might not always have a high

economic value, it can be regarded as a co-product in the organic sys- tems due to the higher dependency on organic fertilizer. To model it as a co-product, information about feed intake at different stages, associated emissions, and fertilizer replacement ratio is necessary. Lastly, to cap- ture the characteristics of organic feeding strategies and housing sys- items, emission factors for N emissions (particularly NH3) need to be adapted. A first effort in adapting these factors to Danish organic housing conditions was performed by Kai et al. (2022). In that study, literature data were screened to project NH3 emission factors for housing systems with outdoor concrete runs. Furthermore, recommendations exist for nutrient modeling at the paddock level, where implications on rotations (the grass-clover and following crop) are considered (Eriksen et al., 2002; Kongsted et al., 2020).

Therefore, the present study aimed to analyze the environmental effect of changes in feeding strategies for organic SOWS and fattening pigs compared to the current standard feeding used in organic herds in Denmark and, at the same time, refine the nutrient modeling approaches for organic pig production taking into account the subsystems and using organic-specific data and emission factors. The changes in feeding strategies included: i) reducing the amount of crude protein in the ra- tions; ii) increasing the proportion of locally produced protein feed while reducing the crude protein in the rations; and iii) reducing the amount of compound feed used (increased efficiency).
9: A cradle-to-farm gate life cycle approach was used to investigate the environmental impacts of feeding strategies in organic pig farming in Denmark. The production chain up to the farm gate was included in the analysis. The following sub-systems were considered i) paddocks with grass-clover for SOWS and piglets (where manure was deposited), including the crops cultivated without additional fertilization after grass-clover; ii) rearing of weaners; iii) rearing of fattening pigs; iv) management of collected manure; v) production of feed; and vi) manufacturing of inputs such as energy (Fig. 1). The piglets weaned in the paddock were transferred to housing units with weaners and then to units with fattening pigs. The manure collected in these housing units was considered a co-product, particularly a currency for (mineral) fer- tilizers in feed production. Furthermore, the utilization of dead animals in the biodiesel and fertilizers industries was comprised in the system boundaries.

The functional unit of the study was 1 kg live weight (LW) delivered at the farm gate and the environmental impacts considered to be influ- enced by the feeding strategies were climate change (CC), eutrophica- tion potential (EP), acidification potential (AP), abiotic depletion of fossil fuels (AD), and land occupation (LO).
10: A cradle-to-farm gate life cycle approach was used to investigate the environmental impacts of feeding strategies in organic pig farming in Denmark. The production chain up to the farm gate was included in the analysis. The following sub-systems were considered i) paddocks with grass-clover for SOWS and piglets (where manure was deposited), including the crops cultivated without additional fertilization after grass-clover; ii) rearing of weaners; iii) rearing of fattening pigs; iv) management of collected manure; v) production of feed; and vi) manufacturing of inputs such as energy (Fig. 1). The piglets weaned in the paddock were transferred to housing units with weaners and then to units with fattening pigs. The manure collected in these housing units was considered a co-product, particularly a currency for (mineral) fer- tilizers in feed production. Furthermore, the utilization of dead animals in the biodiesel and fertilizers industries was comprised in the system boundaries.

The functional unit of the study was 1 kg live weight (LW) delivered at the farm gate and the environmental impacts considered to be influ- enced by the feeding strategies were climate change (CC), eutrophica- tion potential (EP), acidification potential (AP), abiotic depletion of fossil fuels (AD), and land occupation (LO).
11: Animal production data were based on Andersen et al. (2022), where average farm productivity data from 2019 were used (Table 1). In Danish organic pig farming systems, the majority of lactating SOWS are outdoor, in pasture concepts, until the piglets are weaned at a minimum age of seven weeks. The SOWS are indoors for five days after weaning for insemination. The weaners and fattening pigs are housed indoors with partly slatted floors or deep litter in combination with access to outdoor runs with 50% solid/drained floors and 50% slatted floors (Borsting and Hellwing, 2021; Nielsen et al., 2020). This practice is also reflected in the type of roughage used, with the SOWS having an intake of 19% of net energy in roughage (pasture during summer for 180 days and silage during winter) in their rations, while the feed rations for weaners and the fattening pigs include 2.7% and 2.2% silage, respectively (Table 1).
12: Six alternative feeding strategies were considered and compared to the current standard feeding used in organic pig herds in Denmark (Fig. 2). The current feeding was represented by So, the base scenario with feeding as described by Andersen et al. (2022). The changes in the feeding strategy included a reduced amount of crude protein, obtained by changes in the proportion of soy, peas, and barley compared with the standard ration (So). These changes were either only for SOWS (scenario S1) or both for SOWS and fattening pigs (scenario S3). Alternative protein feed types (fava beans and rapeseed cake) that provided the same pro- tein content in rations as in S1 and S3 were investigated in scenario S2 for SOWS and in scenario S4 for both SOWS and fattening pigs. Finally, the effect of increased feed efficiency corresponding to reducing by 10% the amount of compound feed and with the same feed composition as in So was tested for SOWS (scenario S5) and both SOWS and fattening pigs (scenario S6) (Tables 2 and 3). Regardless of the changes in the feeding of compound feed (the amount and type of roughage supply were assumed unaffected across scenarios) all scenarios were carried out assuming no effects on animal productivity as observed by Johannsen et al. (2022), Eskildsen et al. (2020a,b) and Juul et al. (2023; in prep).
13: The organically grown feed items used in the scenarios were pro- duced in three sub-systems i) standard Danish-grown cereals, rapeseed, fava beans, and peas; ii) the crops produced in rotation with the pad- docks for pigs; iii) feed imported to Denmark. The sources used in data collection are presented per parameter and crop considered in Table 4 and further detailed in section S1.1 in the supporting information (S.I.). Regarding the paddock areas, the grass-clover yields were estimated based on experimental data regarding the daily intake of the animals (Eskildsen et al., 2020a; Fernández et al., 2006). Furthermore, because of the manure deposition in these areas, no further fertilization is allowed in Denmark neither in the year with SOWS on the area with grass-clover nor for the cereals grown in the following year in this rotation with grass-clover.

For the imported feed (soybean cake, sunflower cake, wheat bran, fish meal, and vitamins/minerals), environmental impacts from similar conventional-grown feed items were used as best estimates. For soybean cake, sunflower cake, and wheat bran, data for conventionally cultivated crops from Mogensen et al. (2018) were used. Transportation data for soybean cake were however changed to the figures reported by Knudsen

2.2.4. Estimation of emissions and losses At the herd level, enteric methane emissions were estimated following the IPCC (2006a) recommendations based on gross energy data for the animal feed rations compiled by Mikkelsen et al. (2006). Furthermore, methane and N emissions from manure excreted by the animals were determined for housing and storage. For methane,
Danish-specific methane conversion factors (Mikkelsen et al., 2016) and IPCC (2006a) were used.

To estimate NH3 emissions from housing, the factors projected by Kai et al. (2022) for organic Danish pig systems were applied (Table S2). These were used as organic housing systems and feeding strategies differ from those used in conventional systems. For the fattening pigs housed indoors with partly slatted floors and access to outdoor runs with sol- id/drained and slatted floors, Kai et al. (2022) determined NH3 emission factors from the study by Olsson et al. (2014). For the fattening pigs housed indoors with deep litter and access to outdoor runs with sol- id/drained and slatted floors, Kai et al. (2022) adjusted emission factors for deep litter housing in conventional production systems with the NH3

(2010) as organic soybean cake is mainly imported from China. For fish meals, data from Agri-footprint 3.0 database (Durlinger et al., 2017) were used, while for vitamins and minerals, the calcium carbonate process from the Ecoinvent v3 database (Ecoinvent, 2013) was used as a proxy.

2.2.4. Estimation of emissions and losses At the herd level, enteric methane emissions were estimated following the IPCC (2006a) recommendations based on gross energy data for the animal feed rations compiled by Mikkelsen et al. (2006). Furthermore, methane and N emissions from manure excreted by the animals were determined for housing and storage. For methane,
Danish-specific methane conversion factors (Mikkelsen et al., 2016) and IPCC (2006a) were used.

To estimate NH3 emissions from housing, the factors projected by Kai et al. (2022) for organic Danish pig systems were applied (Table S2). These were used as organic housing systems and feeding strategies differ from those used in conventional systems. For the fattening pigs housed indoors with partly slatted floors and access to outdoor runs with sol- id/drained and slatted floors, Kai et al. (2022) determined NH3 emission factors from the study by Olsson et al. (2014). For the fattening pigs housed indoors with deep litter and access to outdoor runs with sol- id/drained and slatted floors, Kai et al. (2022) adjusted emission factors for deep litter housing in conventional production systems with the NH3

losses from the outdoor runs based on the mass of manure N excreted in the two sections (33% indoor and 67% outdoor). For the NH3 losses in outdoor runs, the estimates per m² outdoor slatted floor per year by Aarnink et al. (2015) are matched with organic EU area requirements (1 m² per pig) and Danish productivity data (3.6 production cycles per year) in Kai et al. (2022). The NH3 emission factors regarding the housing of SOWS and weaners are also derived from factors for similar conventional housing systems (Kai et al., 2022). Similar to the fattening pigs, the emission factors for weaners were corrected by Kai et al. (2022) to include both the indoor and the outdoor runs NH3 losses (Table S2). The NH3 emissions regarding manure deposited on paddocks were based on Eriksen et al. (2002). This involved calculation of ammonia emissions based on the feed N input (13% of feed N). Concerning NH3 emissions during storage of manure (Table S2), Danish-specific figures for slurry and deep litter were used (Hansen et al., 2008), which are determined by the technology used in the system (e.g., floating cover, tent cover).
For N2O, the IPCC (2006a) factors were employed to determine emissions per housing type based on the ratio of manure excreted as

slurry and deep litter from Kai et al. (2022). For the manure deposited on the paddock, 8% of the feed N input was estimated as denitrification emissions, as uncovered by Eriksen et al. (2002) (Table S2). Further- more, to determine N2O emissions, a total denitrification nitrous oxide ratio of 2.9:1 was used (Vinther and Hansen, 2004). The N2O emissions during storage were calculated by using IPCC (2006a) factors. An overview of the input, output, and emissions calculated for the consid- ered scenarios is presented in Table 5.

Concerning feed production, foreground emissions were appraised following the methodology by Mogensen et al. (2018, 2014), where the emissions considered were direct N2O from fertilizer application (IPCC, 2006b), NH3 from fertilizer application (Hansen et al., 2008; Mikkelsen et al., 2006), CO2 from liming (IPCC, 2006b), and indirect N2O from NH3 and leaching. The N and P leaching were determined following a mass balance approach, where inputs, outputs, emissions, and soil C changes are quantified and the surplus is expected to be leached to aquatic en- vironments. For soil C changes, 10% of the C input (straw, above-, and below-ground crop residues, manure) to soils was projected to be stored in soils in a 100-years perspective (Mogensen et al., 2014; Petersen et al., 2013).

N leaching rates in the paddocks with SOWS and piglets were also modeled following a mass balance approach. To fully capture the specificities of the systems, the paddock consisted of the area covered with grass-clover that the animals graze on and the area cultivated with unfertilized barley, following the grass-clover, as suggested by Kongsted et al. (2020). Firstly, input-output and losses were determined. The main input to this area was the feed for the animals. Only the feed given to the SOWS and piglets on the paddock was considered. Nevertheless, the input also included the atmospheric N deposition and N fixation for grass-clover, which were set according to Jakobsen et al. (2015). Then, the output comprised the live weight gain of the weaned piglets, the grass clover silage, and barley yield obtained on the fields following the grass-clover when the animals were outdoors. The N content in grass-clover was based on Mogensen et al. (2018). N content in barley was based on feed tables figures from Landbrug og Fodevarer (2022). The NH3 and N20 losses from the manure deposited on the field were

losses from the outdoor runs based on the mass of manure N excreted in the two sections (33% indoor and 67% outdoor). For the NH3 losses in outdoor runs, the estimates per m² outdoor slatted floor per year by Aarnink et al. (2015) are matched with organic EU area requirements (1 m² per pig) and Danish productivity data (3.6 production cycles per year) in Kai et al. (2022). The NH3 emission factors regarding the housing of SOWS and weaners are also derived from factors for similar conventional housing systems (Kai et al., 2022). Similar to the fattening pigs, the emission factors for weaners were corrected by Kai et al. (2022) to include both the indoor and the outdoor runs NH3 losses (Table S2). The NH3 emissions regarding manure deposited on paddocks were based on Eriksen et al. (2002). This involved calculation of ammonia emissions based on the feed N input (13% of feed N). Concerning NH3 emissions during storage of manure (Table S2), Danish-specific figures for slurry and deep litter were used (Hansen et al., 2008), which are determined by the technology used in the system (e.g., floating cover, tent cover).
For N2O, the IPCC (2006a) factors were employed to determine emissions per housing type based on the ratio of manure excreted as

slurry and deep litter from Kai et al. (2022). For the manure deposited on the paddock, 8% of the feed N input was estimated as denitrification emissions, as uncovered by Eriksen et al. (2002) (Table S2). Further- more, to determine N2O emissions, a total denitrification nitrous oxide ratio of 2.9:1 was used (Vinther and Hansen, 2004). The N2O emissions during storage were calculated by using IPCC (2006a) factors. An overview of the input, output, and emissions calculated for the consid- ered scenarios is presented in Table 5.

Concerning feed production, foreground emissions were appraised following the methodology by Mogensen et al. (2018, 2014), where the emissions considered were direct N2O from fertilizer application (IPCC, 2006b), NH3 from fertilizer application (Hansen et al., 2008; Mikkelsen et al., 2006), CO2 from liming (IPCC, 2006b), and indirect N2O from NH3 and leaching. The N and P leaching were determined following a mass balance approach, where inputs, outputs, emissions, and soil C changes are quantified and the surplus is expected to be leached to aquatic en- vironments. For soil C changes, 10% of the C input (straw, above-, and below-ground crop residues, manure) to soils was projected to be stored in soils in a 100-years perspective (Mogensen et al., 2014; Petersen et al., 2013).

N leaching rates in the paddocks with SOWS and piglets were also modeled following a mass balance approach. To fully capture the specificities of the systems, the paddock consisted of the area covered with grass-clover that the animals graze on and the area cultivated with unfertilized barley, following the grass-clover, as suggested by Kongsted et al. (2020). Firstly, input-output and losses were determined. The main input to this area was the feed for the animals. Only the feed given to the SOWS and piglets on the paddock was considered. Nevertheless, the input also included the atmospheric N deposition and N fixation for grass-clover, which were set according to Jakobsen et al. (2015). Then, the output comprised the live weight gain of the weaned piglets, the grass clover silage, and barley yield obtained on the fields following the grass-clover when the animals were outdoors. The N content in grass-clover was based on Mogensen et al. (2018). N content in barley was based on feed tables figures from Landbrug og Fodevarer (2022). The N and P leaching were determined following a mass balance approach, where inputs, outputs, emissions, and soil C changes are quantified and the surplus is expected to be leached to aquatic en- vironments. For soil C changes, 10% of the C input (straw, above-, and below-ground crop residues, manure) to soils was projected to be stored in soils in a 100-years perspective (Mogensen et al., 2014; Petersen et al., 2013).

N leaching rates in the paddocks with SOWS and piglets were also modeled following a mass balance approach. To fully capture the specificities of the systems, the paddock consisted of the area covered with grass-clover that the animals graze on and the area cultivated with unfertilized barley, following the grass-clover, as suggested by Kongsted et al. (2020). Firstly, input-output and losses were determined. The main input to this area was the feed for the animals. Only the feed given to the SOWS and piglets on the paddock was considered. Nevertheless, the input also included the atmospheric N deposition and N fixation for grass-clover, which were set according to Jakobsen et al. (2015). Then, the output comprised the live weight gain of the weaned piglets, the grass clover silage, and barley yield obtained on the fields following the grass-clover when the animals were outdoors. The N content in grass-clover was based on Mogensen et al. (2018). N content in barley was based on feed tables figures from Landbrug og Fodevarer (2022). calculated using the emission factors included in Table S2 for outdoor housing. For barley, fixed emission factors were used for NH3 (2 kg NH3-N and 7 kg N2O/N2-N per ha), as employed by Kongsted et al. (2020). Furthermore, experimental data regarding the fertilizer effect of grass-clover and mineralized N were considered as proposed by Kongsted et al. (2020). Thus, a fertilizer effect for the next crop for grass-clover (1/6 of the field surplus after subtracting the N losses) and a mineralized N rate of 68 kg N per ha were considered as inputs for barley fields following the grass-clover. Lastly, N leaching rates were deter- mined for the whole paddock area (grass clover and barley), but also on average per ha of the grass-clover area (Table 6).

30: 2.2.5.1. Sow-piglet units and handling of dead animals. Economic allo- cation was used to distribute the impacts at the sow-piglet units level between the meat from culled SOWS and the piglets produced. An allo- cation factor of 95.1% was determined for the produced piglets based on average prices from the period 2010-2020 for SOW and piglets from SEGES (2022). Fat and the meat and bone meal produced from the dead animals at the herd level sent to destruction were used in biodiesel and fertilizer production, respectively. Therefore, system expansion was used to account for the avoided production of the above-mentioned products as described by Dorca-Preda et al. (2021).

2.2.5.2. Accounting for manure production and use. Nutrient balance accounting is challenging in (N) modeling of organic systems (Mon- temayor et al., 2022). In this study, nutrient balance was pursued by computing a budget regarding the amount of organic fertilizer produced by the pigs and the amount needed for feed production. The total area requirements of the individual crops were matched with the feed re- quirements, and subsequently, the crops' nutrient requirements (N, P, and K) were matched with the calculated amount of nutrients ex-storage from pig production. When lower quantities of manure ex-storage were available than needed, the extra amount was assumed to be covered by

mineral fertilizers, and the emissions for the production and use hereof were attributed to pig production. If there was a higher quantity of manure than the need for cultivation, the value of the saved commercial manure was credited to pig production. Mineral fertilizer is not allowed in organic production, but this was assumed to be the effect, as manure from conventional systems can be used in organic farming. Thus, the diverging amounts of manure were ultimately reconciled with com- mercial fertilizer. The data regarding the nutrient content in manure for organic systems, substitution rates, energy use, and emissions at appli- cation when using manure to substitute mineral fertilizers are presented in Table S3.
31: 2.2.5.1. Sow-piglet units and handling of dead animals. Economic allo- cation was used to distribute the impacts at the sow-piglet units level between the meat from culled SOWS and the piglets produced. An allo- cation factor of 95.1% was determined for the produced piglets based on average prices from the period 2010-2020 for SOW and piglets from SEGES (2022). Fat and the meat and bone meal produced from the dead animals at the herd level sent to destruction were used in biodiesel and fertilizer production, respectively. Therefore, system expansion was used to account for the avoided production of the above-mentioned products as described by Dorca-Preda et al. (2021).

2.2.5.2. Accounting for manure production and use. Nutrient balance accounting is challenging in (N) modeling of organic systems (Mon- temayor et al., 2022). In this study, nutrient balance was pursued by computing a budget regarding the amount of organic fertilizer produced by the pigs and the amount needed for feed production. The total area requirements of the individual crops were matched with the feed re- quirements, and subsequently, the crops' nutrient requirements (N, P, and K) were matched with the calculated amount of nutrients ex-storage from pig production. When lower quantities of manure ex-storage were available than needed, the extra amount was assumed to be covered by

mineral fertilizers, and the emissions for the production and use hereof were attributed to pig production. If there was a higher quantity of manure than the need for cultivation, the value of the saved commercial manure was credited to pig production. Mineral fertilizer is not allowed in organic production, but this was assumed to be the effect, as manure from conventional systems can be used in organic farming. Thus, the diverging amounts of manure were ultimately reconciled with com- mercial fertilizer. The data regarding the nutrient content in manure for organic systems, substitution rates, energy use, and emissions at appli- cation when using manure to substitute mineral fertilizers are presented in Table S3.

33: The CML (2013) baseline impact assessment method was used to estimate the CC, EP, AP, and AD impacts. Additionally, soil C changes were modeled following the methodology described by Petersen et al. (2013) and Mogensen et al. (2014). For LO, the annual area used for feed production and the area used in the paddock were considered.
34: 3.1. Baseline scenario - standard Danish organic pig production

Standard Danish organic pig production was found to lead to 4.4 kg N20, 41.2 kg NH3, and 54.5 kg CH4 (enteric and manure) emissions per SOW with offspring per year (Table 5). In total, 10.6 t compound feed (74% of total feed used) and roughage was used to produce 2.36 t LW per SOW per year. The average N leaching per ha paddock was 173 kg N (Table 6). This figure corresponds to the average of the fields with an- imals on grass-clover and the fields with barley cultivated after grass- clover (where the excess manure deposited on grass-clover is utilized). According to the estimates by Kongsted et al. (2020) based on eight free-range pig farms in Denmark, the average N surplus for similar areas was 226 kg N per ha, but the figures vary between 90 and 320 kg N per
35: Environmental impacts of standard organic Danish pig production (S0) per kg live weight (LW) in relation to the feeding strategies considered (S1-S6). S1 referred to a reduced amount of crude protein in feed rations of sows; S2 - referred to a reduced amount of crude protein and alternative protein feed sources for sows; S3 referred to a reduced amount of crude protein in feed rations of SOWS and fattening pigs; S4 - referred to a reduced amount of crude protein and alternative protein feed sources for SOWS and fattening pigs; S5 - referred to reduced use of feed for sows; S6 - referred to reduced use of feed for SOWS and fattening pigs.

Environmental impacts|Scenarios So|S1|S2|S3|S4|S5|S6 Climate change (kg CO2 eq. kg LW-1|2.7 (3.1)|2.7 (3.1) 1)|2.6 (3.1) 1)|2.6 (3.1) 1)|2.5 (3.0) 1)|2.6 (3.0) 1)|2.4 (2.9) 1) Eutrophication potential (g PO4 eq. kg LW-1|31.7|31.7|30.1|29.7|29.9|29.6|27.7 Acidification potential (g SO2 eq. kg LW-1|47.1|46.5|46.7|42.4|41.8|45.9|41.2 Abiotic depletion, fossil fuels (MJ kg LW-1|12.6|12.5|12.1|11.8|8.4|12.4|11.0 Land occupation (m ² kg LW-Superscript(1)|8.8|8.8|8.8|8.8|8.4|8.6|8.1
1) The first figure excluded and the figure in brackets included the contribution from soil C changes to climate change.

ha. The figures calculated in the present study (234 kg N per ha) fit in the range described by Kongsted et al. (2020), being similar to the average. With the approach suggested by Kongsted et al. (2020), the effect of the grass-clover on the following crop (barley) and the mineralized N were also accounted for (Table 6). These were determined by the grass-clover (and the deposited manure) but their effect was allocated to barley as also suggested by Avadí et al. (2022) for improving nutrient modeling in LCA.

The above-mentioned data uncovered CC, EP, AP, AD, and LO im- pacts of 2.7 kg CO2 eq., 31.7 g PO4 eq., 47.1 g SO2 eq., 12.6 MJ, and 8.8 m²a per kg LW, respectively (Table 7). When including soil C changes, the CC impact per kg LW increased with 0.45 kg CO2 eq. because of a high percentage of compound feed in animal feed rations (Tables 2 and 3). Unlike perennial crops, the production of annual crops for compound feed leads to releases from the C stocks from soil (Mogensen et al., 2014). Nevertheless, the coefficient used to estimate the amount of below-ground crop residues and then, C input to soil is determined relative to the yield of the crops and developed for conventional crops. This coefficient might need correction for organic farming as Chirinda et al. (2012) found similar or higher root C in organic compared to inorganic fertilizer-based cereal systems despite lower harvested yields in the organic system.

Feed production was the main contributor to CC (without soil C changes), EP, AD, and LO impact categories representing 58, 74, 98, and 100%, respectively, of the total impacts (Figs. 3 and 4). The feed used for fattening pigs had the highest contribution as feed use per kg LW meat is higher for this category compared to the SOWS with piglets or weaners (Tables 2 and 3). The feedstuffs with the highest share in the impact of feed were the cereals and the protein-rich feedstuffs (e.g., soybean cake, fava beans, and peas). For AP, animal production-related emissions (particularly NH3 emissions from manure from fattening pigs) were the hotspot and accounted for 69% of the total impact. The NH3 emissions also contributed to the EP impact (26% of the total impact). The N2O (from manure during housing and storage) and CH4 (from enteric fermentation and manure management) emissions from animal pro- duction (paddock, weaners, and fattening pigs) represented 42% of the total CC impact when excluding soil C changes impact.

The CC figures reported in Table 7 for So are in line with those presented by Andersen et al. (2022). Nevertheless, the approach used in the present study to estimate N emissions at the paddock level deter- mined higher N emissions compared to the approach used by Andersen et al. (2022), which is derived from cattle experimental data. For this reason, EP impact was 11% higher than that reported by Andersen et al. (2022). Andersen et al. (2022) did not include AP in their assessment. Regarding AD, the figures calculated in the present study are 25% higher than those uncovered by Andersen et al. (2022), particularly because of the updates in the AD impacts of soybean cake and vitamins and

minerals. Compared to conventional Danish pork, the CC impact of organic Danish pork is 15% higher when excluding soil C changes and direct land use changes (Dorca-Preda, 2022). However, when including these aspects, the CC of conventional Danish pork is higher with 0.8 kg CO2 eq. per kg LW because of the use of soybean meal produced in Latin America, which is associated with high deforestation rates. Then, while AD impact is similar in organic and conventional (11.1 MJ per kg LW) Danish pig production, the EP, AP, and LO impacts are 2.5, 1.6, and 2.2 times higher, respectively, for organic, compared to conventional pork (Dorca-Preda, 2022). These important differences are explained by higher N emissions and lower productivity in organic animal and feed production.

(2022), which is derived from cattle experimental data. For this reason, EP impact was 11% higher than that reported by Andersen et al. (2022). Andersen et al. (2022) did not include AP in their assessment. Regarding AD, the figures calculated in the present study are 25% higher than those uncovered by Andersen et al. (2022), particularly because of the updates in the AD impacts of soybean cake and vitamins and

minerals. Compared to conventional Danish pork, the CC impact of organic Danish pork is 15% higher when excluding soil C changes and direct land use changes (Dorca-Preda, 2022). However, when including these aspects, the CC of conventional Danish pork is higher with 0.8 kg CO2 eq. per kg LW because of the use of soybean meal produced in Latin America, which is associated with high deforestation rates. Then, while AD impact is similar in organic and conventional (11.1 MJ per kg LW) Danish pig production, the EP, AP, and LO impacts are 2.5, 1.6, and 2.2 times higher, respectively, for organic, compared to conventional pork (Dorca-Preda, 2022). These important differences are explained by higher N emissions and lower productivity in organic animal and feed production.

ha. The figures calculated in the present study (234 kg N per ha) fit in the range described by Kongsted et al. (2020), being similar to the average. With the approach suggested by Kongsted et al. (2020), the effect of the grass-clover on the following crop (barley) and the mineralized N were also accounted for (Table 6). These were determined by the grass-clover (and the deposited manure) but their effect was allocated to barley as also suggested by Avadí et al. (2022) for improving nutrient modeling in LCA.

The above-mentioned data uncovered CC, EP, AP, AD, and LO im- pacts of 2.7 kg CO2 eq., 31.7 g PO4 eq., 47.1 g SO2 eq., 12.6 MJ, and 8.8 m²a per kg LW, respectively (Table 7). When including soil C changes, the CC impact per kg LW increased with 0.45 kg CO2 eq. because of a high percentage of compound feed in animal feed rations (Tables 2 and 3). Unlike perennial crops, the production of annual crops for compound feed leads to releases from the C stocks from soil (Mogensen et al., 2014). Nevertheless, the coefficient used to estimate the amount of below-ground crop residues and then, C input to soil is determined relative to the yield of the crops and developed for conventional crops. This coefficient might need correction for organic farming as Chirinda et al. (2012) found similar or higher root C in organic compared to inorganic fertilizer-based cereal systems despite lower harvested yields in the organic system.

Feed production was the main contributor to CC (without soil C changes), EP, AD, and LO impact categories representing 58, 74, 98, and 100%, respectively, of the total impacts (Figs. 3 and 4). The feed used for fattening pigs had the highest contribution as feed use per kg LW meat is higher for this category compared to the SOWS with piglets or weaners (Tables 2 and 3). The feedstuffs with the highest share in the impact of feed were the cereals and the protein-rich feedstuffs (e.g., soybean cake, fava beans, and peas). For AP, animal production-related emissions (particularly NH3 emissions from manure from fattening pigs) were the hotspot and accounted for 69% of the total impact. The NH3 emissions also contributed to the EP impact (26% of the total impact). The N2O (from manure during housing and storage) and CH4 (from enteric fermentation and manure management) emissions from animal pro- duction (paddock, weaners, and fattening pigs) represented 42% of the total CC impact when excluding soil C changes impact.

The CC figures reported in Table 7 for So are in line with those presented by Andersen et al. (2022). Nevertheless, the approach used in the present study to estimate N emissions at the paddock level deter- mined higher N emissions compared to the approach used by Andersen et al. (2022), which is derived from cattle experimental data. For this reason, EP impact was 11% higher than that reported by Andersen et al. (2022). Andersen et al. (2022) did not include AP in their assessment. Regarding AD, the figures calculated in the present study are 25% higher than those uncovered by Andersen et al. (2022), particularly because of the updates in the AD impacts of soybean cake and vitamins and

minerals. Compared to conventional Danish pork, the CC impact of organic Danish pork is 15% higher when excluding soil C changes and direct land use changes (Dorca-Preda, 2022). However, when including these aspects, the CC of conventional Danish pork is higher with 0.8 kg CO2 eq. per kg LW because of the use of soybean meal produced in Latin America, which is associated with high deforestation rates. Then, while AD impact is similar in organic and conventional (11.1 MJ per kg LW) Danish pig production, the EP, AP, and LO impacts are 2.5, 1.6, and 2.2 times higher, respectively, for organic, compared to conventional pork (Dorca-Preda, 2022). These important differences are explained by higher N emissions and lower productivity in organic animal and feed production.

42: <header>3.2.1. Decreased protein level in feed rations (S1, S3) </header>

When reducing the crude protein content of feed rations for SOWS (S1) and SOWS and fattening pigs (S3), small reductions were found in S1 compared to So (approximately 1% for NH3 and 2% for N2O emissions) (Table 5). More important changes in the level of emissions were observed in S3 with decreases of 12% for NH3 and 9% for N20. Regarding the N leaching rates in the paddocks with SOWS and piglets, a drop of 9% was estimated on average for the whole paddock in S1 compared to So, which involved a reduced N input from feed (Table 6). The decreased protein level used in feed rations in S1 and S3 had very little effect on the emissions of CH4 and the total feed used per SOW with offspring per year (Table 5).

Per kg LW, these changes had more notable effects on EP, AP, and AD impacts in S3 (Fig. 4). The highest effect was observed for AP, where the impact in S3 declined by 10% because of the reductions in the level of NH3 emissions and reductions in the impact of feed for the fattening pigs compared to So. For EP and AD, the contributions from feed for fattening pigs and SOWS with piglets decreased in S3 compared to So causing overall reductions of 6% and 6%, respectively. For the CC impact, small decreases per kg LW were found for the feed used for fattening pigs in S3 compared to So (Fig. 3). Nevertheless, the changes in feed composition also caused an increase of 8% in the soil C impact making the overall CC impact to be similar in S3 and S1. While the contributions from different feedstuffs types changed in S1 and S3 compared to So (the contribution of cereals increased while that of protein-rich feedstuffs decreased partic- ularly in S3), there was no effect on the overall LO impact per kg LW in S1 and S3 compared to So (Fig. 4).

crude protein content in feed rations and the NH3 and N2O emissions per SOW with offspring per year were reduced compared to So, particularly in S4 (Table 5). These reductions were slightly lower than those found for the S3 scenario. Additionally, the total feed used per SOW with offspring

per year increased slightly by 0.9% causing an increase of 0.5% in the level of CH4 emissions per SOW with offspring per year. In terms of N leaching at the paddock level, decreases of 9% were estimated on average for the whole paddock. Similar to the N emissions, the effects on
N leaching were slightly lower for S2 and S4 compared to S1 and S3 (Table

Per kg LW, the effect of S4 was higher than that of S2 compared to So as adjustments were made both for SOWS and fattening pigs. The most important changes were observed for the AD impact, which was the lowest across all scenarios (Fig. 4). These were attributed to decreases in the contribution of feed for SOWS with piglets and fattening pigs to the total impact per kg LW (by 19% for SOWS with piglets and by 44% for fattening pigs). One crucial aspect regarding these reductions regarded the transportation impacts, which were considerably reduced because of using locally grown feedstuffs in feed rations instead of soybean cake imported from China. Feed contribution to the total impact declined also for CC, EP, and LO in S4 compared to So leading to reductions of 3%, 6%, and 5%, respectively. It has to be noted however that for CC, an increase of 16% in the soil C contribution was estimated for the feed used for fattening pigs. Regarding the AP impact, a total decrease of 11% was estimated as a result of decreases in the contributions from feed (6%) and manure management (5%).

Per kg LW, the effect of S4 was higher than that of S2 compared to So as adjustments were made both for SOWS and fattening pigs. The most important changes were observed for the AD impact, which was the lowest across all scenarios (Fig. 4). These were attributed to decreases in the contribution of feed for SOWS with piglets and fattening pigs to the total impact per kg LW (by 19% for SOWS with piglets and by 44% for fattening pigs). One crucial aspect regarding these reductions regarded the transportation impacts, which were considerably reduced because of using locally grown feedstuffs in feed rations instead of soybean cake imported from China. 46: <header>3.2.1. Decreased protein level in feed rations (S1, S3) </header>

When reducing the crude protein content of feed rations for SOWS (S1) and SOWS and fattening pigs (S3), small reductions were found in S1 compared to So (approximately 1% for NH3 and 2% for N2O emissions) (Table 5). More important changes in the level of emissions were observed in S3 with decreases of 12% for NH3 and 9% for N20. Regarding the N leaching rates in the paddocks with SOWS and piglets, a drop of 9% was estimated on average for the whole paddock in S1 compared to So, which involved a reduced N input from feed (Table 6). The decreased protein level used in feed rations in S1 and S3 had very little effect on the emissions of CH4 and the total feed used per SOW with offspring per year (Table 5).

Per kg LW, these changes had more notable effects on EP, AP, and AD impacts in S3 (Fig. 4). The highest effect was observed for AP, where the impact in S3 declined by 10% because of the reductions in the level of NH3 emissions and reductions in the impact of feed for the fattening pigs compared to So. For EP and AD, the contributions from feed for fattening pigs and SOWS with piglets decreased in S3 compared to So causing overall reductions of 6% and 6%, respectively. For the CC impact, small decreases per kg LW were found for the feed used for fattening pigs in S3 compared to So (Fig. 3). Nevertheless, the changes in feed composition also caused an increase of 8% in the soil C impact making the overall CC impact to be similar in S3 and S1. While the contributions from different feedstuffs types changed in S1 and S3 compared to So (the contribution of cereals increased while that of protein-rich feedstuffs decreased partic- ularly in S3), there was no effect on the overall LO impact per kg LW in S1 and S3 compared to So (Fig. 4).

49: When reducing the compound feed intake (kg) of SOWS (S5) and SOWS and fattening pigs (S6) by 10%, the total feed intake (kg) per SOW with offspring per year decreased by 2% and 7% in S5 and S6, respectively. This lowered NH3, N20, and CH4 emissions per SOW with offspring per year by 13%, 9%, and 9%, respectively in S6 compared to So (Table 5). Furthermore, the average N leaching rate per ha paddock in S5 was the lowest across all scenarios due to an increased feed efficiency (Table 6).

Reducing the feed intake for animals corresponded to improved ef- ficiency within the production system, and that was beneficial for all the environmental impacts compared to So, particularly in S6 (Figs. 3 and 4). While the AP impacts in S6 were similar to those estimated for S4, the CC, EP, and LO impacts per kg LW were the lowest across all scenarios. Besides the reductions in the contributions from feed for fattening pigs and SOWS with piglets, the NH3, N20, and CH4 emissions decreased particularly for fattening pigs in S6 compared to So as manure N excreted is dependent on the feed used.
50: When reducing the compound feed intake (kg) of SOWS (S5) and SOWS and fattening pigs (S6) by 10%, the total feed intake (kg) per SOW with offspring per year decreased by 2% and 7% in S5 and S6, respectively. Besides the reductions in the contributions from feed for fattening pigs and SOWS with piglets, the NH3, N20, and CH4 emissions decreased particularly for fattening pigs in S6 compared to So as manure N excreted is dependent on the feed used.
51: In the present study, the emission factors determined by Eriksen et al. (2002) in three commercial organic pig farms in Denmark for NH3 and denitrification were used for paddocks. Furthermore, to estimate emission factors for N20, a denitrification model (Vinther and Hansen, 2004) was used, where a relationship between denitrification and N2O emissions is given. With this approach, N2O emissions represented 2.8% of the feed N intake at the paddock level (Table S2). Other studies analyzing similar farming systems (Horrillo et al., 2020; Zira et al., 2021) followed the IPCC guidelines for soil management (IPCC, 2019, 2006b). The latest IPCC (2019) recommendations include similar factors for ammonia (0.197 kg NH3-N per kg N) and considerably lower factors for N2O (0.4% and 0.6% of manure N for default and wet climates fac- tors, respectively). With the IPCC (2019) approach, the CC impact for So would be 2.5 kg CO2-eq. per kg LW. It must be noted however that these factors are generally derived from grazing ruminant studies (Cai and Akiyama (2016) and other 27 studies), and thus, uncertainty needs to be considered. According to IPCC (2019), the range of variation for N2O emission factors for wet climates is 0-0.026, which fits with the esti- mates by Eriksen et al. (2002). However, more research is needed in free-range pig systems to determine N2O-specific factors.

53: <header>3.3.2. Modeling of N emissions during housing </header>

By using the emission factors determined by Kai et al. (2022), an adjustment of the emission factors to organic production housing con- ditions was carried out. However, Kai et al. (2022) consider these factors to be uncertain because a modeling approach is used to adapt the trial
data provided by Olsson et al. (2014) to Danish conditions. Experi- mental data are necessary to further refine the factors calculated by Kai et al. (2022).
54: Regarding the manure collected during the housing of animals, a co- product modeling approach was employed. Thus, system expansion was performed to account for its fertilizer value. Furthermore, the nutrient requirements of crops were considered to ensure the nutrients are balanced out. According to Montemayor et al. (2022), in the databases including data on organic fertilizer (farmyard manure), the "residual" approach defined by FAO (2016) is used. When using this approach, manure has no economic value to the farmer, but it is valuable in other systems, such as the cultivation of crops. For this reason, manure applied to fields is considered not to have an environmental burden when entering the other production systems. However, the emissions taking place during its utilization in these systems (such as the application of manure) are accounted for.

Since the approach used in the present study quantifies the displacement of mineral fertilizers in crop production (systems that provide inputs to livestock production systems), the outcome is very similar to the "residual" approach per kg LW at the farm gate. With the "residual" approach, the environmental load of organic fertilizers emerges only from its application in fields. In the present approach, the organic fertilizers used in feed production are assumed to have the same burden as mineral fertilizers, but then manure takes the credit for dis- placing the manufacturing of those mineral fertilizers. The main dif- ference arises when there is no balance between the manure collected and the nutrient requirements for feed. Thus, when more manure is produced compared to the nutrient requirements, organic farming sys- tems are credited with the supplementary displacement of fertilizers. For instance, in a system where the nutrient requirements of feedstuffs are 100 kg N and 110 kg N are available ex-storage, the livestock system takes the credit for a supplementary avoided production of 10 kg. If the nutrient requirements of feedstuffs are 100 kg N and 90 kg N are available ex-storage, the farming system needs to outsource conven- tional manure. That is accounted for using mineral fertilizers as a cur- rency. While for nutrient modeling both approaches are appropriate, they both have limitations regarding the modeling of other flows, such as heavy metals. With the present approach, the content of heavy metals in crops needs to be based on average crop rotation data while with the "residual" approach, heavy metal content in manure is modeled based on average statistical data. The choice of using one or another needs to be related therefore to the foreground processes.

56: The six alternative feeding strategies were developed assuming no detrimental impacts on pig productivity. Recent Danish studies on organic free-range SOWS with ad libitum access to grass-clover during summer and to roughage during winter showed that reduced dietary crude protein in the gestational compound feed had no impact on live- born piglets, litter birth weight, or body condition (Eskildsen et al., 2020a, 2020b) and improved the subsequent litter gain (Johannsen et al., 2022). The daily gestational compound SID lysine supplies in the scenarios presented in the current study corresponded to 11.1 g in S1 and S3 and 11.9 g in S5 and S6. These levels exceed the daily compound SID lysine intakes of the protein-restricted SOWS in the study by Johannsen et al. (2022). Thus, the assumption of no negative effects on productivity appears robust for gestating SOWS although long-term studies are needed to exclude effects on longevity.

Reducing protein content in the lactational compound feed is more complex because sufficient protein, particularly SID lysine supply, is essential to support milk production (Feyera and Theil, 2017), and the potential for additional amino acid supply through intake of grass and roughage is limited and with a huge variation in voluntary intake be- tween individuals (Eskildsen et al., 2020a; Kongsted et al., 1999).

Eskildsen et al. (2020a, 2020b) observed that a reduction in lactational protein supply during summer was possible without affecting the litter performance, but in a corresponding winter experiment with gilts only, a reduction in dietary protein compromised milk yield. The latter was due to insufficient compound feed intakes in lactation corresponding to 6.0 kg on days 5-20 and 7.8 kg on days 20-40, which is substantially lower compared to commercial farm practice. Given the national average standards for Danish organic pig farms (Table 1), the daily compound feed supply during lactation amounted to approximately 9 kg compound feed during a 51-day lactation. Even with a reduction in the protein content (S1, S3) or feed supply (S5, S6), the scenarios presented in the current study corresponded to average daily SID lysine supplies of 46 to 49 g from compound feed, which are substantially higher than those obtained through intakes of compound feed and grass in the study by Eskildsen et al. (2020a). Consequently, no negative impacts of the pro- posed strategies on SOW productivity were implied, but undoubtedly, there is a need for on-farm studies reflecting commercial practice to confirm whether a reduction in dietary protein content is possible across reproductive stages, parities, and seasons without compromising SOW productivity and longevity.

Studies on organic fattening pigs revealed that reduced dietary amino acid levels in the compound feed equivalent to 7-14% (Hook Presto et al., 2007) and 15% (Norgaard and Fernández, 2009) below conventional indoor recommendations, had no negative effects on pig performance when the pigs were fed ad libitum. In the scenario with the lowest SID lysine level in the current study (S3), the fattening pigs had an average daily SID lysine intake of approximately 17 g from the com- pound feed. This reflects the daily SID lysine intakes obtained in the above-mentioned studies, which supports the assumption of no negative impacts of the implemented scenarios on the pig performance.
57: The six alternative feeding strategies were developed assuming no detrimental impacts on pig productivity. Recent Danish studies on organic free-range SOWS with ad libitum access to grass-clover during summer and to roughage during winter showed that reduced dietary crude protein in the gestational compound feed had no impact on live- born piglets, litter birth weight, or body condition (Eskildsen et al., 2020a, 2020b) and improved the subsequent litter gain (Johannsen et al., 2022). The daily gestational compound SID lysine supplies in the scenarios presented in the current study corresponded to 11.1 g in S1 and S3 and 11.9 g in S5 and S6. These levels exceed the daily compound SID lysine intakes of the protein-restricted SOWS in the study by Johannsen et al. (2022). Thus, the assumption of no negative effects on productivity appears robust for gestating SOWS although long-term studies are needed to exclude effects on longevity.

Reducing protein content in the lactational compound feed is more complex because sufficient protein, particularly SID lysine supply, is essential to support milk production (Feyera and Theil, 2017), and the potential for additional amino acid supply through intake of grass and roughage is limited and with a huge variation in voluntary intake be- tween individuals (Eskildsen et al., 2020a; Kongsted et al., 1999).

Eskildsen et al. (2020a, 2020b) observed that a reduction in lactational protein supply during summer was possible without affecting the litter performance, but in a corresponding winter experiment with gilts only, a reduction in dietary protein compromised milk yield. The latter was due to insufficient compound feed intakes in lactation corresponding to 6.0 kg on days 5-20 and 7.8 kg on days 20-40, which is substantially lower compared to commercial farm practice. Given the national average standards for Danish organic pig farms (Table 1), the daily compound feed supply during lactation amounted to approximately 9 kg compound feed during a 51-day lactation. Even with a reduction in the protein content (S1, S3) or feed supply (S5, S6), the scenarios presented in the current study corresponded to average daily SID lysine supplies of 46 to 49 g from compound feed, which are substantially higher than those obtained through intakes of compound feed and grass in the study by Eskildsen et al. (2020a). Consequently, no negative impacts of the pro- posed strategies on SOW productivity were implied, but undoubtedly, there is a need for on-farm studies reflecting commercial practice to confirm whether a reduction in dietary protein content is possible across reproductive stages, parities, and seasons without compromising SOW productivity and longevity.

This reflects the daily SID lysine intakes obtained in the above-mentioned studies, which supports the assumption of no negative impacts of the implemented scenarios on the pig performance.

60: Concerning the environmental impact of organic feedstuffs, con- ventional feedstuffs were used as proxies for some of the feedstuffs. Previously conducted research indicates that the carbon footprint of conventional and organic crops is on average relatively similar (Clark and Tilman, 2017; Mogensen et al., 2022). Regarding the other impact categories, organic feedstuffs were found to have higher EP (+37%) and LO (+25-110%) impacts but lower AD (-15%) impacts (Clark and Tilman, 2017). On this background, an underestimation of EP and LO impacts and an overestimation of the AD impacts of feed contribution could be expected for the present results. However, considerable vari- ation exists between and within production systems (e.g., cereals, pul- ses, and oil crops) and could also be found at different production scales (e.g., regional, national, and global) (Clark and Tilman, 2017).
61: In the present study, alternative feeding strategies were analyzed using refined nutrient LCA modeling approaches for organic pig farming. Therefore, specific data regarding the nutrient content of manure were collected and emission factors adapted to organic condi- tions were used for the paddock and housing. Six alternative feeding scenarios for either SOWS or SOWS and fattening pigs were considered and compared to the standard feeding ratios (S0) in organic pig farming in Denmark. These included reduced protein content in feed rations (S1, S3), alternative protein sources and reduced protein content in feed ra- tions (S2, S4), and reduced compound feed intake (S5, S6). These sce- narios led to moderate decreases in the level of N emissions (NH3, N20) and NO3 leaching in the paddock. More considerable reductions in N emissions were found for scenarios S4 and S6, where rations were altered for both SOWS and fattening pigs. These scenarios also had the lowest environmental impacts, with CC (-11% compared to S0), EP (-13% compared to So), AP (-13% compared to S0), and LO (-8% compared to So) being the lowest in S6 and AD (-33% compared to So) being the lowest in S4. The present study contributed to refining nutrient LCA

modeling in organic pig farming by identifying specific nutrient data for manure and suggesting adapted N modeling approaches. Furthermore, the effect of feeding strategies was quantified in relation to several impact categories.
